nativeRange.habitat: Found on shallow tropical reefs, lagoons, and turbid coastal sites, typically at depths of 3 to 25 m. The species tends to favor areas with moderate water movement and mixed lighting conditions.

decorPreferences: large open buffer zone from neighboring corals (15–30 cm minimum)

diet.notes: The Galaxea Coral draws most of its energy from photosynthesis carried out by symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) living within its tissues. Supplemental target-feeding with small meaty foods — such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, copepods, or finely chopped seafood — one to two times per week tends to improve growth rate and coloration, though the coral generally fares well without direct feeding when lighting is adequate. The individual polyps are small (around 6 mm in diameter), so food particles should be appropriately fine.

goodWith: most reef fish (blennies, clownfish, damselfish, dartfish, gobies, jawfish, wrasses)
non-LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle range
avoidWith: other Galaxea colonies (have been known to attack conspecifics)
any coral within 15–30 cm sweeper tentacle reach
large hermit crabs that could damage the brittle skeleton

summary: The Galaxea Coral (Galaxea fascicularis) is a large polyp stony (LPS) coral widely regarded as one of the most territorially aggressive species in the hobby, capable of extending its sweeper tentacles 15–30 cm at night to sting neighboring corals. It is generally recommended for experienced reefkeepers who can provide ample isolation space.
careNotes:
The Galaxea Coral (Galaxea fascicularis) is widely regarded as one of the most territorially aggressive large polyp stony (LPS) corals in the reef-keeping hobby. Its sweeper tentacles are reported to extend 15–30 cm (6–12 inches) or more at night — sometimes described as extending up to twenty times the daytime length — making it capable of stinging and killing neighbouring corals at what appears to be a safe distance during the day. Placement planning must account for nighttime reach, not just daytime polyp extension.

Key care requirements:

• Placement: Generally advised to position in the lower-to-middle area of the rockwork, on an elevated ledge or isolated rock that provides a 15–30 cm buffer zone in all directions from other corals or sessile invertebrates. Because the sweeper tentacles are largely invisible or retracted during the day, new keepers commonly underestimate the spacing required.

• Lighting: Tends to do well under moderate PAR (100–200 μmol/m²/s). Acclimate new specimens to lower light first and raise gradually; sudden high-intensity exposure has been known to cause bleaching. LED, T5, and metal halide fixtures all appear to be tolerated when positioned appropriately.

• Flow: Moderate, turbulent, non-laminar flow is generally preferred. Strong direct laminar flow directed at the coral can cause polyps to retract and may contribute to tissue recession. Variable gyre-style flow tends to allow the polyps to sway gently and filter feed effectively.

• Skeleton fragility: The skeleton is notably brittle compared to many other LPS corals. Physical knocks or drops can crack the skeleton and expose live tissue to infection. Brown jelly disease is a documented risk following physical trauma; affected areas should be removed promptly and flow around the specimen increased.

• Fragging: Possible using a bone cutter, wire saw, or frag saw, but requires careful handling. New frags should be positioned well away from established corals. Attach to a frag plug or rubble and allow to heal under moderate flow.

• Aggression toward conspecifics: Two Galaxea colonies placed near each other have been documented attacking one another. A single specimen per tank section (or per tank in smaller systems) is generally recommended.
breedingNotes: Fragging (the process of cutting a coral into smaller pieces to propagate it) is the standard method of reproducing the Galaxea Coral in captivity. Because the skeleton is notably brittle, a wire saw or fine-bladed bone cutter is recommended over coarser tools to reduce the risk of cracking and tissue tearing. New frags should be kept well isolated from established colonies while they heal. In the wild, the species reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practised in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – CCGC Aquacultured Green Long Polyp Galaxea Coral
primary.notes: Product page confirmed: scientific name Galaxea astreata (congener of G. fascicularis with identical husbandry); common names Tooth Coral, Star Coral, Crystal Coral, Starburst Coral, Brittle Coral, Galaxy Coral; temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12; care level Moderate; flow Medium; lighting Moderate to High; highly aggressive sweeper tentacles extending 'up to several inches' at night.

additional.name: AquariumSource – Galaxea Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked all high-stakes fields. Temperature 75–82°F (24–28°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, Ca 380–450, Mg 1250–1350; PAR 100–200; flow moderate; minimum tank 50 gal; sweeper tentacles 6–12 inches at night. Care level intermediate. Feeding 2–3×/week meaty foods.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquariumBlog – Galaxea Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temp 76–81°F (24.4–27.2°C), pH 7.2–8.4 (lower pH bound appears anomalous — all other sources show 8.1 minimum; retained 8.1 as consensus), SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–11, Ca 400–450, Mg 1200–1350. Depth range 3–25 m. Colony size up to 2 m in wild, ~30 cm in aquarium. Highly aggressive; sweeper tentacles 15–30 cm. Minimum tank 50 gal (189 L). Fragging confirmed.

additional.name: FishLaboratory – Galaxea Coral Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (up to 3 ft wild, 12 in aquarium), PAR 50–150, flow low-to-moderate (slightly more conservative than other sources; moderate adopted as consensus), care level moderate, minimum tank 50 gal. Confirmed sweeper tentacle reach described as 'up to 20× daytime length.' Confirmed native range Indo-Pacific and Red Sea, depth <60 ft (~18 m).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Galaxea (taxonomy and biology)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation: Family Euphylliidae, Order Scleractinia. Confirmed sweeper tentacles with 'powerful cnidocytes.' Native range Indo-Pacific and Red Sea. Depth <20 m. Not used for husbandry parameters.
